Name: Puka Nacua
Outlook: Puka was dominant in 2025 with a league-leading 107 receiving yards per game and six weeks of 20+ fantasy points, the most among WRs. He was an absolute dominator on 50/50 balls, tracking down 27 contested catches, the most of any WR over the last decade. Aside from off-the-field issues, he is simply unstoppable and off-the-charts in every per-route metric we care about for fantasy. If you wanted to take Puka at the 1.01 in a full PPR league, we totally understand.
Dynasty: Money please! That sound is the giant bag of cash coming Puka’s way after he totaled the second-most receiving yards through a WR’s first three years in the NFL behind only Justin Jefferson. We have him firmly amongst an elite three of WRs we would take at the very beginning of dynasty startups.

Name: Ja'Marr Chase
Outlook: Let us hit you with some hard-hitting analysis. Ja’Marr Chase is good at the game! Even with Joe Burrow missing a huge chunk of the season, the dude still finished as the WR4 with over 15 fantasy points per game. Finishing as the WR1 overall is obviously in play, especially if Burrow can finally stay healthy and have one of those elite TD seasons. He is well worth the pick early in Round 1 of fantasy drafts.
Dynasty: In our opinion, Chase is still viewed as one of those few untouchables in dynasty formats. Despite already being in the league for five years, he is still just 26 years old and at the peak of his prime. We have him locked in as a no-brainer top-five overall asset in dynasty.

Name: Jaxon Smith-Njigba
Outlook: If you grabbed JSN in the third round last year, you likely won your league as Smith-Njigba was incredible all year long, leading the league in receiving with almost 1,800 yards and 10 scores. His efficiency could regress after he averaged a silly 3.74 yards per route run, but he is still an easy click in the first round of fantasy drafts as one of the few guys in the league who can command a 30+% target share.
Dynasty: Seattle just made JSN one of the highest-paid WRs in the league, and we know the talent is immense. He is an easy top-five WR moving forward and well worth a first-round selection in start up drafts.

Name: Amon-Ra St. Brown
Outlook: St. Brown is the pinnacle of consistency. He has now finished as a top-8 fantasy WR in four straight seasons, and he has banked the WR3 overall finish in three straight years as Jared Goff’s high-volume WR1. There aren’t many WRs we would want over him in redraft leagues this season, making him a rock-solid pick in Round 1 of drafts.
Dynasty: St. Brown is locked in with the Lions through the 2028 season, and he is still just 26 years old. We expect the fantasy goodness to continue for the next two to three years at a minimum, making him one of the most valuable WRs in all of dynasty.

Name: CeeDee Lamb
Outlook: Lamb had a good season in 2025, but it didn’t feel great considering you likely had to take him in the first round last year. He missed a few games because of the high ankle sprain, and he only scored three TDs. In the game we play, that is just not going to get it done. However, he is coming off a career high 14.4 yards per reception and has now finished with 1,000+ yards in five straight seasons. If that TD variance swings back in his direction, CeeDee can absolutely finish as a top-5 WR in 2026.
Dynasty: Lamb remains one of the most valuable WRs in all of dynasty, but it is at least worth considering that he is 27 years old. He will remain ultra-productive for the next couple of years, so if you have him, keep taking that top-tier production. Just have it on your radar for the next two years that the trade window might close more quickly than you think. We aren’t saying he will fall off any time soon, but we generally prefer to be a year early in order to maximize returns in the trade market.

Name: Justin Jefferson
Outlook: JJetts disappointed fantasy managers who grabbed him early in drafts last season, but was it really his fault? I mean, I think we all know who to blame here…“Nine” was one of the worst starting QBs in football, and you may forget, but Carson Wentz (yes, that guy) started five games while Max Brosmer attempted to play football in the NFL. Spoiler: It did not go well. Basically, we are completely ignoring Jefferson’s 2025 season. He is still one of the best in the game, and while Kyler is not a top-five QB, he is certainly an upgrade on whatever the heck the Vikings were doing in 2025. He is one of fantasy’s most obvious bounce-back players for 2026 and is well worth a look in Round 1 of drafts.
Dynasty: Jefferson is still one of the most valuable WRs in dynasty formats despite his down 2025 campaign, and we are confidently taking him in the first round of startup drafts.

Name: Drake London
Outlook: Can we get this man a QB? We know London is one of the most talented WRs in the league, yet it feels like there is still so much untapped potential given what has been going on in Atlanta. Even though Tua and Penix have their warts, we are closing our eyes in drafts and betting on talent with Drake, who has been targeted on 30% of his routes over the last two seasons. In case you missed it, the Falcons are trotting out some combination of the three stooges behind London: Jahan Dotson, Zachariah Branch, and Olamide Zacchaeus. Let’s get this guy 10+ targets a game and see what happens!
Dynasty: In dynasty, betting on young, highly drafted, and productive WRs is a winning strategy long term. That is why we will continue to bet on London even if the surrounding environment leaves a lot to be desired. Let’s hope the Falcons can find their long-term franchise QB next offseason because the Drake is here to stay. He signed a massive 4-year extension with the team in early June, making him one of the highest paid WRs in the league.

Name: Nico Collins
Outlook: Even with C.J. Stroud disappointing more often than not from a real-life NFL perspective, Collins continues to post WR1 numbers on a per-game basis. He is just that dude in this offense, and with Tank Dell recovering from a major injury and last year’s rookies, Jayden Higgins and Jaylin Noel, unproven commodities, Collins’ elite target share remains locked in. He is on the short list of players who are capable of finishing as a top-five fantasy WR in 2026.
Dynasty: Nico is now 27 years old, so he has a couple more seasons in his prime. If you are a contender, you are obviously riding this out and taking the production, but if you are a rebuilding team, we do not mind putting him on the trade block and getting a haul. Our philosophy in dynasty is that we would generally prefer to be a year early rather than a year late.

Name: A.J. Brown
Outlook: You heard about it for months and it finally happened... A.J. Brown is now a Patriot.  As for the player himself, last year probably felt frustrating as Philly’s offense sputtered and he finished with career lows in yards per reception (12.9). Even in a “down” year, Brown still finished as fantasy’s WR10 and an efficient 2+ yards per route run for his 7th consecutive season. He remains one of the league's best in man coverage as an absolute bully in one-on-one situations. Perhaps a change of scenery with Drake Maye could unlock Brown for another WR1 finish in 2026.
Dynasty: We have had Brown as a trade-away candidate in the Dynasty Pass. It is not because we think he will have a bad season in 2026, but rather, we would prefer to maximize value after his NFL trade in the dynasty trade market a year early. You can likely still get a young WR plus a first-round rookie pick if you are willing to move off the soon-to-be 29-year-old vet.

Name: Chris Olave
Outlook: Olave was buried in ADP a year ago due to concerns about the Saints' QB room and his concussion history. Fantasy drafters who took the plunge on Olave in the middle rounds last season were rewarded in a big way as Olave banked career highs in targets (156), receptions (100), yards (1,163), and TDs (9) en route to a WR6 finish. Shough for the win! After Spencer Rattler was benched, Olave went on a tear, averaging 15.8 points per game with Tyler Shough under center. The target competition will be more difficult with the addition of Jordyn Tyson, but Olave is one of the most talented WRs in the league, and we love Kellen Moore’s up-tempo scheme. We would not be shocked to see Olave post back-to-back WR1 seasons.
Dynasty: Olave should be able to cash in a huge payday from the Saints in the very near future, as he is entering the final year of his rookie deal after New Orleans picked up his fifth-year option. With the Saints trying to build around Tyler Shough on a cheap rookie contract, Olave’s extension should come sooner rather than later, giving him stability as a fringe WR1 in dynasty start-ups.

Name: George Pickens
Outlook: George! Jason’s My Guy came through in a huge way in his first season in Dallas. His stats are a bit juiced for those games without CeeDee Lamb, but the point remains - Pickens is a super talented WR who plays in a scheme we like with a QB in Dak who can support elite fantasy seasons. The only thing to consider for fantasy is that the ADP for him is higher than it was a season ago, so we are not getting any discounts here like we were a year ago when Pickens was available in the middle rounds.

Does he end up getting a huge extension with Dallas, or does he go chase the bag elsewhere in 2027? The upside is obviously there to be a top-12 to top-15 option, but the situation isn’t stable right now, given the contract question marks.

Name: Tetairoa McMillan
Outlook: McMillan delivered on the promise of being a top-10 NFL Draft pick a year ago, finishing with just over 1,000 yards and seven scores. The draft capital, Year 1 production, and efficiency marks suggest McMillan is poised for another step forward in his sophomore campaign. After seeing 25.4% of the target share a year ago as a rookie, T Mac is on our short list of WRs who could push for upwards of a 30% market share. With his talent, that should mean great things for fantasy.

He broke out as a rookie, has elite draft capital, and has very little target competition on his own team. Name: Garrett Wilson
Outlook: All this dude does is earn targets at an elite level. Since 2023, G Willy’s targets per route run rates are 25%, 25%, and 26%. Simply put, he is one of the best in the game at commanding a high target share. Unfortunately, that hasn't always translated into elite fantasy seasons, but we attribute that to lackluster QB play compared to other NFL WR1s. *Geno Smith has entered the chat.* Sadly, it could be the exact same scenario for Wilson in 2026. The talent is there, and the targets will be there, but the offensive environment could hold him back for fantasy once again. Nevertheless, he is a rock-solid WR2 for your team and gets a boost in full PPR formats.
Dynasty: If we can get this man a real QB, he could break fantasy. Until that problem is solved (maybe in 2027?!), his name will probably hold more value on your dynasty squad than the actual fantasy production. He is entering his age-26 season, and he signed a massive $130 million extension not that long ago, tying him to New York through the 2030 season.

Name: Emeka Egbuka
Outlook: Respectfully, Gronk. What the heck, dude?! Before Gronk called his man Ee-Meek-Ah Eg-Big-Boo, Egbuka was on fire, operating as the WR1 in Tampa en route to a true rookie year breakout. After Gronk’s snafu on live TV, Egbuka fell off the map, averaging just 3.2 catches and 41.1 yards per game from Week 6 on. If you zoom out, however, there are a lot of reasons for that - Egbuka played through a minor hammy injury, Baker got banged up, the O-line was injured, and Emeka’s catchable target rate was terrible. If drafts present an opportunity to buy back in on the former first-round pick, we are ready to pounce, especially with Mike Evans now in San Francisco and Chris Godwin another year older.

He is young, he is a former first-round pick who averaged 1.75 yards per route run, and he showed he can play in this league. Godwin may not be on this team much longer, so if there is any small buying window in your league on Egbuka, thanks to his up-and-down rookie year, we are definitely interested in seeing what it costs to get him on your dyno squad.

Name: DeVonta Smith
Outlook: Smitty has always been a great talent at the WR position, but his fantasy value has been held in check with AJ Brown on the roster. With Brown gone, expect "Slim Reaper" to be one of the hottest names in fantasy. Smith will have a chance to set career highs in targets, receptions, and yards as the Eagles’ new WR1. His ADP will probably skyrocket, but the excitement is warranted for a guy who’s averaged 14.5 PPR points per game without Brown in the lineup going back to 2022.
Dynasty: Smith has always had the talent to produce like a WR1, but he has been pigeon-holed as the second option in Philly ever since the Eagles traded for AJ Brown. That should change in a big way in 2026. Expect Smith’s dynasty value to skyrocket, but just be aware that he is already turning 28 years old later this year. He is still in his prime for now, but the window is probably closing faster than you might realize.

Name: Tee Higgins
Outlook: Blurb- “Meat Higgins,” as he is known around Fantasy Footballers HQ, has now finished as a top-24 fantasy WR in four of the last five years as the Robin to Ja’Marr Chase’s Batman. As long as Joe Burrow stays healthy, we expect both guys to eat this year, with Higgins the more volatile option of the two Cincy WRs. We are fine with Higgins as a pick where he goes as a WR2 for your squad, but he has always got that sneaky WR1 upside if Chase happens to miss time.
Dynasty: Higgins still has three more years left on the contract extension that he signed before the 2025 season, and he is still just 27 years old. He likely has at least two to three more great seasons in him before we talk about a potential decline. Keep riding this thing out, especially if you are a contender.

Name: Rashee Rice
Outlook: Welp! Another offseason discussing Rashee Rice and his off-the-field issues. Rice missed the first six weeks of the 2025 season while serving a suspension, and this past offseason, he violated his probation when he tested positive for marijuana. As a result, he is serving jail time in June and July. On top of that, we also got news in late May that Rice underwent a “clean up” procedure in his knee that could sideline him for roughly two months. If you recall, Rice’s 2024 season came to an end after he suffered a season-ending LCL, hamstring and posterolateral corner injury. So, to recap, we have another legal issue and a health concern. It all adds up to make Rice a highly volatile player entering training camp, assuming he is cleared. When on the field, he produces like a WR1 for fantasy, but there are obviously concerns. We will be tracking his situation closely over the next few months.
Dynasty: If you are a Rice manager in dynasty, this latest off-the-field incident feels like a complete gut punch. He is so valuable in terms of his fantasy production, but he is in a contract year and likely losing trust with the Chiefs. If they aren’t willing to pay him, and he leaves the Andy Reid and Pat Mahomes scheme, does his future production take a hit, and what happens if he is in the news again for legal troubles? His dynasty value could take a massive hit in the next 12 months.

Name: Jameson Williams
Outlook: You know what is fun? Jamo ripping off chunk gains thanks to some of that elite speed. Dan Campbell knows this. John Morton? Not so much. Before Guns Mahoney took over play calling, Jameson Williams’ role was frustrating for fantasy as he was used as a low-volume, high aDOT boom/bust type of guy. However, after Campbell took over the offense, Jameson Williams was one of fantasy’s most valuable WRs as he was on pace for 1,400 yards after the Week 8 bye. It is important to note that Sam LaPorta was also out for a big chunk of the season. Still, the point remains: Jamo is crucial to the Lions’ offense reaching its full potential, and he should continue to have a high upside role that is worth targeting in fantasy drafts.
Dynasty: Williams just signed a massive three-year extension with the Lions, so he is locked in with Detroit through 2029. It is possible he never turns into a consistent WR1 for fantasy, but he is an extremely valuable dynasty asset who can win you a week when those big plays hit.

Name: Christian Watson
Outlook: Watson’s bug-a-boo since he entered the NFL has been injuries. When he is on the field, he is highly efficient and scores plenty of fantasy points. That was on full display last season when he returned to the lineup in Week 8 off a torn ACL and went on to average 2.67 yards per route run and 11.5 fantasy points per game. The Packers’ depth chart is thinned out entering 2026, and Matthew Golden really struggled as a rookie, giving Watson relatively weak target competition for one of the better offenses in the league. There is a shaky floor with a guy like Watson due to durability concerns, but if you are looking for a high upside WR pick in the middle rounds, Watson can be nitro to your squad.
Dynasty: The Packers gave Watson a huge 4-year, $110 million contract extension in early June. They view him as a bonafide WR1 with this type of money but we have him in the low-end WR2 territory in dynasty.

Name: Zay Flowers
Outlook: Flowers is coming off a highly efficient 2025 campaign, setting a career high in targets (118), receptions (86), and yards (1,211). Over the last two seasons, Zay has averaged 2.61 and 2.35 yards per route run. Those are some elite marks. The guy is good! Unfortunately, the one bug-a-boo for Zay in fantasy land has been TDs. Through three seasons, he has topped out at just five scores. In order to make the leap into being a real difference maker, he is going to need to find the painted box more often. Perhaps he can fall into an outlier TD year, but with Lamar stealing some attempts in the red zone and King Henry still here, that seems somewhat unlikely for 2026.
Dynasty: Flowers is in his prime, productive, and tied to one of the better offenses in the league. What’s not to like?! We are in on Zay in dynasty formats, viewing him as a top-24 guy. However, we aren’t sure if he can truly level up to being a top-12 type of asset. The ceiling feels capped, but he is a rock-solid WR to have on your dyno squad.

Name: Terry McLaurin
Outlook: After being one of the most valuable WRs in fantasy in 2024, Scary Terry’s 2025 was a year to forget. Not only did McLaurin deal with injury throughout the season, but the Commanders also lost Jayden Daniels for a significant chunk of the year. To no one’s surprise, the Commanders’ offense was stuck in the mud in what was essentially a lost season. Now, looking forward to 2026, McLaurin has a chance to bounce back in a big way. The depth chart here behind McLaurin is barren, so it wouldn’t be surprising at all to see Terry push for a healthy target share and 8-10 TDs if things break right.
Dynasty: McLaurin got a new deal from Washington last year, but he is entering the stage of his career where you should expect some gradual decline over the next couple of seasons. He will be 31 later this year, so if you are not a contender, it is time to shop him to the rest of your league. If you are a top team in your league, we are fine riding it out and taking the production in 2026.

Name: Malik Nabers
Outlook: When on the field and healthy, there aren’t many better than Malik Nabers. Unfortunately, the talk all offseason about Nabers is going to be injury, specifically his recovery from ACL and meniscus surgery. It is one of fantasy football’s biggest storylines to monitor throughout the summer. We are projecting him to be limited early in the season, so just understand that Nabers will come with risk in redraft leagues, and it is not out of the question that he could start the season on the PUP list. If and when he does get back to 100%, he is likely to provide a big impact to your team, but you might need to wait longer than you would prefer.
Dynasty: Nabers is a young superstar. Those guys are really hard to acquire in dynasty leagues, but this injury might provide a small buying window. If you are not a contender and can take on some risk with your sights set on the future, Nabers is a great trade target, knowing you could really get a bounce-back season in 2027.

Name: Jaylen Waddle
Outlook: For the first time in his career, Jaylen Waddle is going to be wearing a different uniform as he is now in Denver after being traded before the NFL Draft. The situation is clearly much better for scoring, playing with Sean Payton and Bo Nix versus Bobby Slowik and Malik Willis. The talent remains with Waddle, so there is upside here, but be mindful that Payton historically loves to use multiple WRs, RBs, and TEs to spread things out. We would love to say Waddle will dominate targets, but the more likely scenario is that Waddle will have to have a highly efficient season to be a difference maker for fantasy.
Dynasty: Waddle’s recent seasons have not been what we had hoped for after some excellent fantasy seasons to start his career. He has been banged up, Tua got injured and regressed in a big way, and Tyreek Hill was stealing plenty of targets in Miami. Perhaps a change of scenery will bring Waddle back to fantasy dominance, but it is also possible we have already seen the best season of his career. This time next year, he will be 28 years old, so if you are not a contender and can flip Waddle on the intrigue of the Broncos offense, it might not be a bad idea.

Name: Rome Odunze
Outlook: Last season was a rollercoaster for Rome Odunze. It feels like forever ago, but remember when Rome was THE WR1 in the first month of the season? The guy was on fire to open the year, but after the team’s bye, he regressed and then got injured, missing Weeks 14-18 with a foot injury. Now entering 2026, there is perhaps a buying window in fantasy as the market is hot and heavy for Luther Burden and Colston Loveland. This is still a former top-10 draft pick, and he is guaranteed to play in 2WR sets for one of the best play-callers in the league. Burden and Loveland feel like the more exciting fantasy picks in Chicago, but Rome outscoring both of those guys is in the range of outcomes.

It looked like he put it all together in the first month of the season, but injuries certainly derailed his 2025 season. There is a chance the manager in your league is a bit frustrated, making him an interesting trade target heading into 2026.

Name: Mike Evans
Outlook: For the first time in his career, Mike Evans won’t be wearing a Bucs uniform. Weird! He is now in San Francisco as he chases another ring with the Niners. Historically speaking, there is an increased risk with veterans changing teams at this stage of their career. It was a tough 2025 season for the elder statesman, who missed a ton of time with hamstring and collarbone injuries. He will be 33 this season, so be mindful of his ADP throughout the summer. Name: Davante Adams
Outlook: Adams is coming off arguably the best season ever for a WR inside-the-five as he became the team’s “goal-line WR”. He saw the most targets (16) inside the 5-yard line since targets became a stat (1992). Nevertheless, the warning signs are there as he totaled just 789 receiving yards and now enters his age-33 season. When we couple that with Matthew Stafford’s outlier TD season, it is hard to be bullish on Adams repeating as a WR1 for fantasy. We view him more as a WR3, as his price tag in drafts is a bit too rich for us.

Name: Ladd McConkey
Outlook: We love us some Ladd, but his sophomore season was a relative disappointment, regressing in every major statistical category compared to his WR11 finish as a rookie. He particularly struggled versus zone coverage (1.31 YPRR) as a predominantly slot WR (65% of routes) while ceding meaningful targets to old man Keenan Allen. It remains to be seen if he will be consistently on the field in 2WR sets as new OC Mike McDaniel historically adds a fullback on the field to mess with WR usage. Quentin Johnston and Tre’ Harris will likely see a ton of snaps for blocking purposes and potentially limit some of his red zone opportunities. Name: DK Metcalf
Outlook: Pittsburgh traded for Metcalf, then signed him to a huge deal…only to target him 99 times. What? Why? Needless to say, it was a very frustrating 2025 for Metcalf managers last season in that Arthur Smith/Aaron Rodgers offense. On one hand, Mike McCarthy’s offense should be much more throw-heavy in 2026, but on the other hand, Metcalf is now competing with Michael Pittman for targets and still has Aaron Rodgers or an inexperienced youngster as his QB. Name: Luther Burden III
Outlook: Last year’s second-round pick didn’t do much early on, but when he got his chance, he capitalized, particularly during Chicago’s playoff run. The Bears are obviously counting on Burden to take a big step forward this season after trading DJ Moore to the Bills, but it takes a lot of projection to buy into Burden’s juiced-up ADP. He is the hotness right now in Chicago’s WR room, but we need to remember he only earned 60 total targets for a 12.8% target share as a rookie. A bet on Burden is a bet on the talent profile and Ben Johnson using him to his full potential. Those efficiency marks signal some serious talent, but admittedly, there is still some uncertainty given the small sample size.

Name: Carnell Tate

Name: Brian Thomas Jr.
Outlook: What happened here?! BTJ was amazing as a rookie, and all signs pointed to a huge breakout in Year 2. Unfortunately, Thomas’ second season in the league was plagued by inconsistency, drops, injury, and poor play overall. Ultimately, he ended up being one of fantasy’s biggest busts of 2025. Entering 2026, we are now looking at this Jags’ depth chart completely differently. Travis Hunter’s usage remains unclear, Parker Washington played so well down the stretch that you would think he has earned a role, and the team traded for Jakobi Meyers last year, then immediately signed him to an extension. Something’s gotta give in a crowded WR room. Right now, we are viewing BTJ as a potential bounce-back candidate on one of the better offenses in the league. Because of last year’s headache and the crowded WR room, his ADP is depressed, so if you are looking for a volatile mid-round option who could pay off in a big way, BTJ is worth a swing in the middle rounds.
Dynasty: There aren’t many WRs who go out and do what Brian Thomas did as a rookie. Is there cause for concern after a frustrating Year 2? Sure, but this is still a former first-round NFL WR who averaged 2.56 yards per route run as a rookie. Over the last decade, most of the guys who check both of those boxes have gone on to be fantasy difference-makers. We recognize the risk, but now that his cost is down in startups and in the trade market, it might be time to see what it takes to get him on your squad.

Name: Alec Pierce
Outlook: Get that money, Mr. Pierce! After years of being a part-time role player for the Colts, Pierce showed out in 2025 with his first 1,000-yard season and cashed a huge payday with Indy. That financial commitment from the team suggests they think he can level up as a top target without Michael Pittman, but it is worth noting that he has never been a high-volume pass catcher, even going back to his college days, so it does take some projection on that front. For context, he has never had more than 52 receptions in a single season in either the pros or in college. There is some volatility with a big play threat like Pierce, so we like pairing him with more reliable high target share guys earlier in your draft.
Dynasty: It took a few years for Pierce to develop in the NFL, so the fact that he went from a bench roster clogger to a guy who is one of the highest-paid WRs in the league is kind of wild. Nonetheless, this massive contract gives him plenty of value in dynasty formats. He is an interesting player with a wide range of outcomes, so we could see this going either direction. If you are a dynasty player who likes to play the market, it is certainly possible he has already reached peak value, so we would not fault anyone for seeing what they can get in a trade for Pierce after that huge contract.

Name: Marvin Harrison Jr.
Outlook: Alright, Marv. Listen up, buddy. It is make-up or break-up time. We have defended you on our show for a couple of seasons, and Mike even wrote a song about the pants store in your honor! Fantasy gamers were hoping for that classic Year 2 breakout a season ago, but it was anything but. Marv had another lackluster season, with this one defined by inconsistency and injuries that limited him to just 12 games. Through two seasons, he is averaging just 9.3 fantasy points per game. As the former fourth-overall pick, that is just not good enough. The QB room remains a giant question mark, and Trey McBride is the alpha here. Unfortunately, we are at a point where Marv has settled into a boom/bust fantasy option, but the good news is we expect his ADP to slide quite a bit this year compared to previous seasons, and this is a brand new scheme. Is the risk already baked into the price?
Dynasty: Based on our studies, most WRs who become difference makers do so by Year 3. If you took Marv with the first overall pick in your rookie draft two seasons ago, it is important to reset your expectations. Through two years, he averaged 1.74 and 1.68 yards per route run. Those are solid efficiency marks, but they are middling for a former top-five overall pick. If Marv can’t break out this year, he is probably settling into WR3ville for fantasy.

Name: Quentin Johnston
Outlook: Only five WRs collected 8+ receiving TDs in each of the last two seasons: Ja’Marr Chase, Amon-Ra St. Brown, Tee Higgins, Davante Adams, and (drumroll)… Quentin Johnston. After previously being the laughingstock of fantasy leagues, he morphed into a solid outside option for Justin Herbert. He led the team in receiving yards (789), deep targets (19), and fantasy points per game (10.4). He may not look appealing on a fantasy roster, but Johnston’s TD upside makes him a worthy second FLEX option, especially in games where the Chargers are favored. He averaged a solid six targets and 11 fantasy points per game in wins the last two seasons
Dynasty: The Chargers picked up his fifth-year option, so Quge will be on the team for at least the next two years. He is a cheap FLEX option worth acquiring as a throw-in for trades.

Name: Parker Washington
Outlook: Washington went from just a depth piece for Jacksonville to their leading receiver in 2025. Wild times, man! Following the bye week and Travis Hunter’s injury, Washington saw a 23% target share while running roughly half his routes from the slot. We are really interested in taking shots on this Jaguars’ offense, but it is nearly impossible to project the WR usage here, especially with Travis Hunter’s role up in the air. As a result of that uncertainty, expect the Jaguars guys to all be decent values in drafts. There is some downside with each individual WR here, but if you hit on the right guy, the payoff could be massive, especially at these depressed ADPs.
Dynasty: A former sixth-round NFL Draft pick who didn’t do much early in his career, there is a good chance you were able to snag Washington off your waiver wire. If you did, that is a massive boost for your squad to add a guy who can give you WR2 or WR3 production in the right matchups. He is entering the final year of his rookie deal, so it remains to be seen if he gets an extension. The team invested a first-round pick in BTJ a couple of years back, traded up to select Travis Hunter second overall just a year ago, and signed Jakobi Meyers to a three-year deal after trading for him. It is possible Washington is the odd man out after 2026, making his dynasty evaluation really difficult.

Name: DJ Moore
Outlook: It is not difficult to get excited about one of Josh Allen’s top weapons. We do see a world where Moore has a nice season in Buffalo if he can command WR1 usage. That said, in 2024, Allen became the first top-5 fantasy QB without a top-36 scoring WR or top-12 TE. In 2025, he did it again. Joe Brady’s offense can function without hyper-targeting a single WR. Moore’s efficiency marks dropped off in 2025 (just 1.33 YPRR), so we are not exactly sure if he is the same guy he was a few years back. Perhaps a change of scenery will serve him well, but there is a wide range of outcomes here.
Dynasty: Moore has been on the Hitman’s Dynasty Trade Away list all off-season. Will he be productive for Josh Allen and the Bills this year? Yes. However, the time to capitalize in the trade market is right now, before the bottom falls out. Moore is already 29 years old, and generally speaking, this is right about the age where veteran players start to decline. If you can sell on the hype of Josh Allen’s WR1, we are doing that now as we see Moore’s value taking a big hit in 2027 and beyond.

Indy’s possession receiver over the last handful of years, Pittman should operate in a similar role in Pittsburgh and offers a nice compliment to the more explosive DK Metcalf. Given the state of the QB room (an inexperienced younger guy or an aging Aaron Rodgers), we are skeptical of the ceiling here, but Pittman projects as a high-floor WR3/FLEX option for 2026 given his target-earning ability.
Dynasty: Pittman has been a solid WR for years in the NFL, but in dynasty circles, his name no longer carries a ton of value as he is set to enter his age-29 season. Despite Pittsburgh signing him to a brand new contract this offseason, we are not thrilled with this team’s outlook in the short or long term, given the lack of stability at the QB position. He is a nice depth piece if you are a contender, but you are not winning your dynasty league because of Pitty City.

Name: Josh Downs

That is the million-dollar question for a guy like Downs, who could see a big uptick in playing time and targets with Michael Pitman Jr. now in Pittsburgh. Name: Michael Wilson
Outlook: Michael Wilson’s 2025 breakout was…nuts. We almost never see nonproductive WRs pop up for huge games the way Wilson did in 2025, but here we are. The man went nuclear without Marvin Harrison Jr. on the field, averaging seven catches and 97 receiving yards per game from Weeks 11-18. For context, that 17-game pace is 187 targets and over 1,600 yards. Fantasy drafters need to be aware that the Jacoby Brissett pass rate of last season is probably going to regress, and with Marv now healthy, we likely will not see the same target share. That said, there is a world that exists where Wilson is more productive than Marv in 2026. Pain.
Dynasty: Wilson AKA Jerry Rice went from a dynasty roster clogger to a weekly must-start down the stretch last year. Truth be told, we are not sure what to make of Wilson’s story. He was a fine, yet uninspiring prospect, then spent 2.5 years running wind sprints before doing what he did in 2025. He is in a contract year, so the future is murky at best. If you can capitalize in the trade market in your league after his hot run to close 2025, it is probably not a bad idea. We see a wide range of outcomes moving forward.

Name: Ricky Pearsall

After getting shot just weeks before the start of his pro career in 2024, Pearsall dealt with a PCL injury and an ankle injury a season ago, limiting him to just nine games. As a result, we are still left wondering how good this guy even is as an NFL WR. For fantasy, we have a wide range of outcomes here. Name: Jerry Jeudy
Outlook: Is Jeudy a capable NFL WR? Sure. Is he frustrating to watch due to the inconsistency? Yep. Is he a fun click in fantasy? Nope! Judge Jeudy had that magical stretch with Jameis Winston in 2024 (#JameisForever), but outside of that small sample, he has largely been irrelevant for fantasy since he arrived in Cleveland. The Browns took multiple rookie WRs in this draft, so his playing time isn’t a guarantee moving forward, especially with a new coaching staff in town.
Dynasty: Jeudy’s dynasty value was already in free fall before the Browns double-dipped in the NFL Draft with KC Concepcion and Denzel Boston. Jeudy comes from the previous regime, so Todd Monken may prefer to get a look at the young guns. That leaves Jeudy in no man’s land when it comes to his future outlook. He is under contract through 2027, but it wouldn’t be surprising to see his name in trade rumors.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Jayden Higgins
Outlook: Higgins had a quiet rookie season, going over 50 receiving yards in a single game just three times. It is difficult to be relevant in fantasy football when you are averaging just four targets per game and a 12.3% target share while playing for an offense that isn’t exactly lighting it up on a routine basis. There is room for those market share numbers to grow in Year 2, but it is worth noting that Tank Dell might return to the lineup, Xavier Hutchinson is also part of this team, and Jaylin Noel could also see some more snaps this season as well. Right now, Higgins is nothing more than a late-round dart in redraft leagues with the hopes that he increases his route share in his second season and C.J. Stroud bounces back to support multiple fantasy-relevant WRs.
Dynasty: Houston took Higgins with the 34th overall pick in last year’s draft, so they are still going to be counting on him as he enters his second season in the league. His Year 1 peripherals aren’t amazing, but they aren’t terrible either. In the three games where he posted an 80+ percent route share, he scored double-digit PPR points in all three of those games, and on the season, he averaged a respectable 1.45 yards per route run. Those numbers leave the light on that Higgins can develop as a pro, but obviously, there is a wide range of outcomes here moving forward.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Antonio Williams
Outlook: Terry McLaurin figures to be option A, B, and C in Washington. Behind him, this depth chart is yucky. Deebo Samuel is gone, and the Commanders did very little to replace him in free agency. Enter Williams, who has a chance to be the #2 option right away as a rookie. One question mark on Williams is how his game will translate to the NFL after he ran almost 80% of his routes from the slot in college. We expect him to start out as the team’s primary slot WR, and hopefully, he can jump into 2WR sets. If not, his ceiling is probably capped for fantasy. Even so, his ADP is so late that we don’t mind taking a shot on him in drafts and stashing him to see how things play out early in the season.
Dynasty: The Commanders didn’t have a second-round pick in the NFL Draft. After going defense seventh overall, they used their third-round pick on Clemson’s Antonio Williams. He broke out right away as a true freshman but failed to really level up and become a dominator in college. Still, there is some signal that perhaps he can produce right way as a rookie, given the state of the depth chart. He is one of our favorite later-round targets in both dynasty and redraft leagues for 2026.

Name: Malik Washington
Outlook: Who is the WR1 for the Dolphins? And does it matter? Man, this Miami depth chart is something else. I mean, what a crew. We honestly do not feel confident in playing time or target shares here because this is a brand-new regime with brand-new weapons and a brand-new QB. The Dolphins project to be one of the most run-heavy teams in the league under a defensive-minded head coach and run-first OC Bobby Slowik. For now, Washington looks like he will have a role as the slot WR in this offense, but we have seen enough of him in the NFL to know that a low aDOT, gadgety player isn’t going to be a difference maker for fantasy, especially in an offense that could be the worst in football.
Dynasty: Washington comes from the previous regime, and with Miami taking multiple rookies in the NFL Draft, there is a good chance Washington is on the outside looking in when we get to the end of the season. He carries next to no value in dynasty leagues. Sorry, Malik!

Dynasty: At age 31, on a restructured deal, and injuries at the forefront, it is time to move on from him in dynasty.

Name: Adonai Mitchell
Outlook: AD Mitchell has always had that tantalizing upside, but he is entering Year 3 in the NFL, and it still remains to be seen if he can play as an every-down complete NFL WR. After being sent to New York via trade last year, Mitchell popped up for some big games down the stretch without Garrett Wilson, but in classic AD fashion, he disappeared for weeks at a time. He will now have a full offseason in the system, so perhaps he can put it all together in 2026. Unfortunately, the target competition is now pretty difficult with Wilson healthy, Breece Hall in the backfield, and the Jets double-dipping in the first round of the NFL Draft with Kenyon Sadiq and Omar Cooper Jr. You do not need to be drafting AD in your redraft leagues, but if Wilson misses time again, he could be a plug-and-play option off the waiver wire.
Dynasty: Mitchell’s slowly entering “You can’t cut that guy” territory. Basically, he is good enough to be on your dynasty squad, and you can always talk yourself into holding him with the hopes he develops, but you know the odds are stacked against him after two lackluster years in the league. Through two seasons, he is averaging just 3.9 targets per game and is already on his second team. Historically, that is not a recipe for long-term success in dynasty land.

He is not fantasy relevant right now, but if one of the WRs goes down to injury, we at least know he can play.
Dynasty: Dike is a dart throw/end-of-bench stash in dynasty formats. Obviously, his outlook is capped with Carnell Tate going fourth overall and Wan’Dale signing that big contract in free agency. Perhaps he can carve out the WR3 role over time.

Name: Zachariah Branch
Outlook: We know Drake London is the dude here in Atlanta, but behind him, this depth chart is wide open. Dynasty: If Branch is going to work for fantasy in the long run, he is going to have to be an outlier. He is an undersized speed WR, and 45% of his career targets in college came behind the line of scrimmage, with over 35% of his career yards coming via screens. Yikes! This is the exact type of profile that has burned dynasty players in the past, but when you look at the state of this depth chart behind Drake London, you can squint and see a path. We consider him a dart-throw type of stash in dynasty formats.

Name: Malachi Fields
Outlook: The Giants traded multiple picks to land Notre Dame’s Malachi Fields, so the new staff likely has a role in mind for him. At 6’5” and 218 pounds, he is a true X receiver. Unfortunately, for fantasy, Fields looks like a guy who is more likely to help the Giants than help our fantasy teams, as he is a pretty weak prospect coming out of Notre Dame. We know Malik Nabers is the alpha when healthy, and the team paid big money to Isaiah Likely in free agency. Sarratt is a ball winner who was frequently used in the red zone in college, giving him life as a contributor right away as a rookie in Baltimore. That said, we expect Flowers and Bateman to open the season as the top WRs on the depth chart, and the Ravens have never had a high pass rate in the Lamar Jackson era, so the path for fantasy is pretty thin out of the gate. Dynasty: Baltimore double-dipped in the NFL Draft, taking Ja’Kobi Lane in Round 3, then Sarratt in Round 4. Even though the Ravens took Lane first, you could argue Sarratt is the better prospect for the game we play. Not only did he catch 15 TDs for the National Champion Hoosiers, but he also had a 2.56 career yards per route run rate and was second in the entire class behind only Makai Lemon in first downs per route run.

Dynasty: From a dynasty outlook, the draft capital here is doing A LOT of the heavy lifting. Analytically, Stribling is a pretty weak prospect. One area where he does excel is after the catch, which is usually what the 49ers look for in their WRs. He is also good in the blocking game…sure sounds like a Kyle Shanahan receiver, doesn’t it? All in all, we are not expecting much in Year 1, and analytically, it is an uphill battle for this to work.
